content:
First, rephrase the problem in graph‐theoretic terms.  Think of the \(1000\times1000\) board as the grid graph \(P_{1000}\times P_{1000}\): its vertices are the unit‐squares and two vertices are joined by an edge exactly when the corresponding squares share a side.  A domino covering two adjacent squares is then an edge of this graph, and the condition “no two dominoes are adjacent” means exactly that no two chosen edges may share a vertex or even be joined by an edge.  Equivalently, we are looking for a maximum *induced* matching in the \(1000\times1000\) grid.

A very natural way to guarantee the “no‐adjacency’’ condition is to partition the board into disjoint \(2\times2\) blocks and place exactly one domino in each block.  Since a \(2\times2\) square has exactly two horizontal edges and two vertical edges, and any two of those lie either sharing a vertex or sharing an edge, you can put at most one domino in each \(2\times2\).  Conversely, you *can* choose exactly one (say, the top two squares) from every \(2\times2\) block, and those dominoes will not touch each other along an edge.

How many such \(2\times2\) blocks fit in a \(1000\times1000\) board?  Well, you chop each side into 500 consecutive pairs, so you get
\[
\frac{1000}{2}\times\frac{1000}{2}\;=\;500\times500\;=\;250\,000
\]
blocks, and hence you can place 250 000 dominoes.  

It remains only to argue that no larger arrangement is possible.  One way to see this is via a simple counting‐and‐pigeonhole bound: each domino covers 2 squares and (forbids by adjacency) at least 2 more squares in its own \(2\times2\) cell from ever being used by a *second* domino in that cell.  Thus each \(2\times2\) block can contain *at most* one domino, giving the upper bound of \(250\,000\).  

Therefore the maximum number of non‐adjacent dominoes that one can place is

\[
\boxed{250000}.
\]

Think of the \(1000\times1000\) board as the grid graph \(P_{1000}\times P_{1000}\): its vertices are the unit‐squares and two vertices are joined by an edge exactly when the corresponding squares share a side.  A domino covering two adjacent squares is then an edge of this graph, and the condition “no two dominoes are adjacent” means exactly that no two chosen edges may share a vertex or even be joined by an edge.  Equivalently, we are looking for a maximum *induced* matching in the \(1000\times1000\) grid.

A very natural way to guarantee the “no‐adjacency’’ condition is to partition the board into disjoint \(2\times2\) blocks and place exactly one domino in each block.  Since a \(2\times2\) square has exactly two horizontal edges and two vertical edges, and any two of those lie either sharing a vertex or sharing an edge, you can put at most one domino in each \(2\times2\).  Conversely, you *can* choose exactly one (say, the top two squares) from every \(2\times2\) block, and those dominoes will not touch each other along an edge.

How many such \(2\times2\) blocks fit in a \(1000\times1000\) board?  Well, you chop each side into 500 consecutive pairs, so you get
\[
\frac{1000}{2}\times\frac{1000}{2}\;=\;500\times500\;=\;250\,000

It remains only to argue that no larger arrangement is possible.  One way to see this is via a simple counting‐and‐pigeonhole bound: each domino covers 2 squares and (forbids by adjacency) at least 2 more squares in its own \(2\times2\) cell from ever being used by a *second* domino in that cell.  Thus each \(2\times2\) block can contain *at most* one domino, giving the upper bound of \(250\,000\).

\[

\]
